1:
"This then was the lot of the tribe of the children of Judah by their families; even to the border of Edom the wilderness of Zin southward was the uttermost part of the south coast." The witnesses note that this was the lot of the tribe of Judah, the first and chief inheritance divided in the land — the crux of the inheritance given by lot, the portion of God's appointment. On "this then was the lot of the tribe of the children of Judah by their families," interpreters expound the lot. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the inheritance by lot — "this then was the lot of the tribe of the children of Judah by their families; even to the border of Edom the wilderness of Zin southward": Judah, the chief tribe, received the first lot in the promised land, its border reaching to Edom and the wilderness of Zin; the witnesses note the significance — the division of the inheritance by lot, the portion of each tribe assigned not by man's will but by God's appointment through the lot (Judah first and foremost, the royal tribe, given the first and largest portion, the lot the means by which God allotted the inheritance); the lesson — that the inheritance of God's people is of his appointment, the lot disposing each portion, "the lot is cast into the lap; but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD," Proverbs 16:33; the truth that God assigns to his people their place and portion, the inheritance his gift and not man's choice, "the lines are fallen unto me in pleasant places; yea, I have a goodly heritage," Psalm 16:6; the figure — the portion appointed by God, fulfilled in the inheritance of the saints, each having his place in the kingdom by God's allotting, "to give an inheritance among all them which are sanctified," Acts 20:32; the truth that Judah, the tribe of the promise and of the King, received the chief portion, a figure of the pre-eminence of Christ's kingdom; the figure of the inheritance distributed by God to each according to his will; the call to rest content with the portion God appoints; the assurance that God gives his people a goodly heritage. The figure of the inheritance given by lot, the portion of God's appointment.

On "this then was the lot of the tribe of the children of Judah by their families," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the lot of Judah. Keil and Ellicott note Judah, the chief tribe, receiving the first lot, the inheritance assigned by the lot of God's appointment (the inheritance by lot, Proverbs 16:33) — the lot (of Judah).

On "even to the border of Edom the wilderness of Zin southward was the uttermost part of the south coast," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the border. Calvin and Gill note the southern border of Judah's portion reaching to Edom and the wilderness of Zin, the inheritance bounded as God allotted, a figure of the portion appointed to the saints (the goodly heritage, Psalm 16:6; Acts 20:32) — the figure (the inheritance distributed by God to each according to his will); the providence (the lot, the bounds of the south border to follow, vv.2-4). The verse displays the lot of Judah, the first and chief inheritance.

The witnesses agree the substance: Judah, the chief tribe, received the first lot, its border reaching to Edom and the wilderness of Zin (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the inheritance by lot: the lot of Judah — the division of the inheritance by lot, the portion of each tribe assigned by God's appointment, Judah first and foremost given the chief portion, the lot the means by which God disposed the inheritance (Proverbs 16:33), God assigning to his people their place and portion as a goodly heritage (Psalm 16:6), a figure of the inheritance of the saints, each having his place by God's allotting (Acts 20:32). The verse displays the inheritance-by-lot crux. The verse marks the lot: of Judah. The verse displays the inheritance: assigned. The verse displays the portion: appointed. The verse displays the saints: each his place. The lot displays the inheritance given by God's appointment. The verse displays Judah: first. The verse displays the border: to Edom. The verse displays the heritage: goodly. The lot of Judah, the first and chief inheritance, displays the crux: this then was the lot of the tribe of the children of Judah by their families (Proverbs 16:33; Psalm 16:6; Acts 20:32) — the inheritance divided by lot, the portion of God's appointment, Judah the chief tribe given the first portion, a figure of the inheritance of the saints each having his place by God's allotting. The verse displays the bounds of Judah's south border, from the salt sea to Kadeshbarnea (vv.2-4).
2:
"And their south border was from the shore of the salt sea, from the bay that looketh southward:" The very brief verse notes the start of Judah's south border at the salt sea. The grammatical voices note the border. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "their south border was from the shore of the salt sea, from the bay that looketh southward" — the south border of Judah began at the bay of the salt sea; the and their south border was from the shore of the salt sea (the salt sea); the from the bay that looketh southward (the southern bay); the figure (the border of the inheritance carefully bounded, the portion marked out; the truth that the bounds of Judah's portion were set by God's appointment; the figure of the inheritance precisely bounded, each tribe's portion defined; the south border at the salt sea; the inheritance marked, vv.1). The verse holds together the south border.

On "And their south border was from the shore of the salt sea," the witnesses note the border. Gill notes the border at the salt sea (the bound) — the border (the salt sea).

On "from the bay that looketh southward," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the bay: "from the bay that looketh southward" — the southern bay of the sea (the bound); the figure (the inheritance precisely bounded); the providence (the border, the way to Akrabbim and Zin to follow, vv.3). The verse displays the start of Judah's south border at the salt sea.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the border — "their south border was from the shore of the salt sea, from the bay that looketh southward," the border (Keil, Gill); "And their south border was from the shore of the salt sea," the border (Gill); "from the bay that looketh southward," the bay (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the border. The verse marks the salt sea: the start. The verse displays the bay: southward. The verse displays the portion: bounded. The verse displays the inheritance: marked. The border displays the inheritance precisely bounded. The verse displays the bound: set. The verse displays the south: marked. The verse displays the portion: defined. The start of Judah's south border at the salt sea displays the inheritance precisely bounded: their south border was from the shore of the salt sea, from the bay that looketh southward — the south border of Judah beginning at the bay of the salt sea, the portion carefully bounded by God's appointment. The verse displays the border passing to Akrabbim and on to Zin (vv.3).
3:
"And it went out to the south side to Maalehacrabbim, and passed along to Zin, and ascended up on the south side unto Kadeshbarnea, and passed along to Hezron, and went up to Adar, and fetched a compass to Karkaa:" The very brief verse continues the south border to Kadeshbarnea. The grammatical voices note the line. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "it went out to the south side to Maalehacrabbim, and passed along to Zin, and ascended up on the south side unto Kadeshbarnea... and fetched a compass to Karkaa" — the border ran south past Akrabbim to Kadeshbarnea and around to Karkaa; the and it went out to the south side to Maalehacrabbim, and passed along to Zin (past Akrabbim to Zin); the and ascended up on the south side unto Kadeshbarnea, and passed along to Hezron, and went up to Adar, and fetched a compass to Karkaa (to Kadeshbarnea and Karkaa); the figure (the border traced point by point, the portion exactly bounded; the truth that the line of Judah's inheritance was carefully marked; the figure of the inheritance precisely defined, the bounds set; the border by Kadeshbarnea; the portion marked, vv.2). The verse holds together the south border.

On "and ascended up on the south side unto Kadeshbarnea, and passed along to Hezron, and went up to Adar, and fetched a compass to Karkaa," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the points: "ascended up on the south side unto Kadeshbarnea... and fetched a compass to Karkaa" — the border to Kadeshbarnea and Karkaa (the bounds); the figure (the inheritance precisely defined); the providence (the line, the border to the river of Egypt to follow, vv.4). The verse displays the south border to Kadeshbarnea.

The verse displays the border passing to Azmon and the river of Egypt to the sea (vv.4).
4:
"From thence it passed toward Azmon, and went out unto the river of Egypt; and the goings out of that coast were at the sea: this shall be your south coast." The very brief verse completes the south border at the river of Egypt and the sea. The grammatical voices note the bound. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "from thence it passed toward Azmon, and went out unto the river of Egypt; and the goings out of that coast were at the sea: this shall be your south coast" — the south border reached the river of Egypt and ended at the sea; the from thence it passed toward Azmon, and went out unto the river of Egypt (to the river of Egypt); the and the goings out of that coast were at the sea: this shall be your south coast (ending at the sea); the figure (the south border completed at the sea, the portion fully bounded; the truth that the southern line of Judah was traced to its end; the figure of the inheritance bounded on every side, the portion complete; the south coast defined; the inheritance marked, vv.3). The verse holds together the south coast.

On "From thence it passed toward Azmon, and went out unto the river of Egypt," the witnesses note the river. Gill notes the border to the river of Egypt (the bound) — the river (of Egypt).

On "and the goings out of that coast were at the sea: this shall be your south coast," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the end: "the goings out of that coast were at the sea: this shall be your south coast" — the border ending at the sea (the south coast); the figure (the inheritance bounded on every side); the providence (the coast, the east and north borders to follow, vv.5). The verse displays the south border at the river of Egypt and the sea.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The verse displays the east border at the salt sea and the north border (vv.5).
5:

And their border in the north quarter was from the bay of the sea at the uttermost part of Jordan" — the east border was the salt sea to the end of Jordan, and the north border began at the bay of the sea; the and the east border was the salt sea, even unto the end of Jordan (the east border at the salt sea); the and their border in the north quarter was from the bay of the sea at the uttermost part of Jordan (the north border); the figure (the east and north borders bounded, the portion marked on every side; the truth that the inheritance of Judah was defined east and north; the figure of the inheritance bounded on every side, the portion complete; the borders set; the inheritance marked, vv.4). The verse holds together the east and north borders.

The verse displays the east border and the start of the north border.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the borders — "the east border was the salt sea... 6:

7:
"And the border went up toward Debir from the valley of Achor, and so northward, looking toward Gilgal, that is before the going up to Adummim, which is on the south side of the river: and the border passed toward the waters of Enshemesh, and the goings out thereof were at Enrogel:" The very brief verse continues the north border by the valley of Achor toward Enrogel. The grammatical voices note the line. The verse holds together the north border.

The verse displays the border by the valley of Hinnom and the south of Jerusalem (vv.8).
8:
"And the border went up by the valley of the son of Hinnom unto the south side of the Jebusite; the same is Jerusalem: and the border went up to the top of the mountain that lieth before the valley of Hinnom westward, which is at the end of the valley of the giants northward:" The very brief verse notes the north border by the valley of Hinnom and Jerusalem. The grammatical voices note the line. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the border went up by the valley of the son of Hinnom unto the south side of the Jebusite; the same is Jerusalem... at the end of the valley of the giants northward" — the border ran by the valley of Hinnom past the south of Jerusalem; the and the border went up by the valley of the son of Hinnom unto the south side of the Jebusite; the same is Jerusalem (by Hinnom and Jerusalem); the and the border went up to the top of the mountain that lieth before the valley of Hinnom westward, which is at the end of the valley of the giants northward (to the mountain and the valley of the giants); the figure (the border by the valley of Hinnom, later the place of abomination, and Jerusalem, the city of the great King; the truth that the line passed the south of the Jebusite city, later Jerusalem; the figure of the inheritance bounded by the place that would be the holy city, "beautiful for situation... the city of the great King," Psalm 48:2; the border by Jerusalem; the inheritance marked, vv.7). The verse holds together the border by Jerusalem.

On "And the border went up by the valley of the son of Hinnom unto the south side of the Jebusite; the same is Jerusalem," the witnesses note the city. Gill notes the border by Hinnom past the south of Jerusalem (the city, Psalm 48:2) — the city (Jerusalem).

The verse displays the north border by the valley of Hinnom and Jerusalem.

9:

10:
"And the border compassed from Baalah westward unto mount Seir, and passed along unto the side of mount Jearim, which is Chesalon, on the north side, and went down to Bethshemesh, and passed on to Timnah:" The very brief verse continues the north border by mount Seir to Timnah. 13:
"And unto Caleb the son of Jephunneh he gave a part among the children of Judah, according to the commandment of the LORD to Joshua, even the city of Arba the father of Anak, which city is Hebron." The witnesses note that Caleb received his portion, Hebron, according to the commandment of the LORD — the crux of the reward of faith, the inheritance promised to the faithful and given by God's word. On "unto Caleb the son of Jephunneh he gave a part among the children of Judah, according to the commandment of the LORD," interpreters expound the gift. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the reward of faith — "and unto Caleb the son of Jephunneh he gave a part among the children of Judah, according to the commandment of the LORD to Joshua, even the city of Arba the father of Anak, which city is Hebron": Caleb received Hebron, the city of Arba the giant, as his portion, given according to God's command, the reward of his faith and full following of the LORD; the witnesses note the significance — the faithfulness of God in keeping his promise to Caleb, who wholly followed the LORD when the other spies failed, now given his promised inheritance by God's express command (the portion not by lot but by the commandment of the LORD, the reward of the faith that had stood when others quailed, "because he hath another spirit with him, and hath followed me fully," Numbers 14:24); the lesson — that God rewards the faith and faithfulness of his servants, keeping his word to them, the promise made forty-five years before now fulfilled (the long-deferred promise kept, the reward of perseverance, "thy land whereon thy feet have trodden shall be thine inheritance," Joshua 14:9); the truth that God is faithful to reward those who fully follow him, the promise sure, "he is faithful that promised," Hebrews 10:23; the figure — the inheritance given to the faithful, fulfilled in the reward of those who endure and follow Christ fully, "to him that overcometh will I grant to sit with me," Revelation 3:21; the truth that the place of the giants, the hardest portion, was given to the man of strongest faith, who feared not the Anakim; the figure of the reward of faith that claims the promise and possesses it; the call to follow the LORD fully and inherit the promise; the assurance that God keeps his word to the faithful. The figure of the reward of faith, the inheritance promised to the faithful and given by God's word.

On "and unto Caleb the son of Jephunneh he gave a part among the children of Judah, according to the commandment of the LORD to Joshua," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the portion by God's command. 14:
"And Caleb drove thence the three sons of Anak, Sheshai, and Ahiman, and Talmai, the children of Anak." The witnesses note that Caleb drove out the three sons of Anak from Hebron — the crux of the victory of faith over the giants, the very terrors that had made the unbelieving spies quail. On "Caleb drove thence the three sons of Anak, Sheshai, and Ahiman, and Talmai," interpreters expound the conquest. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the victory of faith — "and Caleb drove thence the three sons of Anak, Sheshai, and Ahiman, and Talmai, the children of Anak": Caleb drove out the three giant sons of Anak, the very Anakim whose name had once terrified the spies; the witnesses note the significance — the triumph of Caleb's faith over the giants, the conquest of the very terror that had made the ten spies and the people quail and turn back (the same Sheshai, Ahiman, and Talmai, the sons of Anak, that the spies had reported, "the children of Anak, which come of the giants... we were in our own sight as grasshoppers," Numbers 13:33, now driven out by the man who had believed God); the lesson — that faith overcomes the very terrors that unbelief flees, Caleb taking the giants' stronghold where unbelief had despaired, "if God be for us, who can be against us?" Romans 8:31; the truth that the faith that follows God fully conquers what unbelief dreads, the giants no obstacle to the believing, "this is the victory that overcometh the world, even our faith," 1 John 5:4; the figure — the conquest of faith over its greatest terrors, the believer overcoming the powers that dismay the faithless; the truth that what the flesh calls impossible, faith possesses, the giants driven out by the old warrior at eighty-five; the figure of the victory of faith that fears no giant and claims the promise; the call to face the terrors of the way in faith; the assurance that faith overcomes the giants. The figure of the victory of faith over the giants, the terrors that unbelief had fled.

On "Give me a blessing; for thou hast given me a south land; give me also springs of water... he gave her the upper springs, and the nether springs," interpreters expound the request. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the asking of the fuller blessing — "who answered, Give me a blessing; for thou hast given me a south land; give me also springs of water. And he gave her the upper springs, and the nether springs": Achsah, given a dry south land, asked her father for springs of water, and received both the upper and the nether springs; the witnesses note the significance — the holy boldness of the daughter who asks her father for the fuller blessing, not content with the dry land but seeking the springs that would make it fruitful, and receiving more than she asked (the south land barren without water, the springs the very thing that gives it worth, the father giving both upper and nether springs, abundance beyond the request); the lesson — that the children of God are to ask of their Father the full blessing, the springs of living water, not content with the bare inheritance, and that he gives liberally and beyond their asking, "ask, and it shall be given you," Matthew 7:7; the truth that the Father gives good gifts to his children who ask, and gives abundantly, "able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think," Ephesians 3:20; the figure — the soul thirsting for the springs of living water, the fuller blessing of the Spirit, "whosoever drinketh of the water that I shall give him shall never thirst," John 4:14; the truth that the dry portion is made fruitful by the springs, the blessing of God that waters the inheritance; the figure of the believer who asks for and receives the upper and nether springs, the blessings of heaven and earth; the call to ask boldly for the fuller blessing; the assurance that the Father gives more than we ask. The figure of the soul that asks of the Father the fuller blessing and is not denied.

On "who answered, Give me a blessing; for thou hast given me a south land; give me also springs of water," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the request. Keil and Ellicott note Achsah asking her father for a blessing and springs of water, not content with the dry south land but seeking the springs that would make it fruitful (the asking of the fuller blessing, Matthew 7:7) — the request (springs of water).

On "and he gave her the upper springs, and the nether springs," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the abundant gift. Calvin and Gill note Caleb giving both the upper and the nether springs, more than she asked, a figure of the Father who gives abundantly above our asking, the springs of living water that water the dry inheritance (the abundant gift, Ephesians 3:20; John 4:14) — the figure (the soul thirsting for the springs of living water); the providence (the gift, the cities of Judah in the south to follow, vv.20-21). The verse displays Achsah's request for springs and Caleb's gift of the upper and nether springs.

The witnesses agree the substance: Achsah asked her father for springs of water, and received both the upper and the nether springs (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of asking the fuller blessing: give me also springs of water — the holy boldness of the daughter who asks her father for the fuller blessing, not content with the dry land but seeking the springs, and receiving more than she asked, the children of God asking the full blessing of their Father, who gives liberally and abundantly (Matthew 7:7; Ephesians 3:20), a figure of the soul thirsting for the springs of living water, the fuller blessing of the Spirit (John 4:14), the dry portion made fruitful by the springs. The verse displays the fuller-blessing crux. The verse marks Achsah: asking. The verse displays the springs: given. The verse displays the Father: giving abundantly. The verse displays the living water: sought. The request displays the soul that asks the Father the fuller blessing. The verse displays the dry land: watered. The verse displays the gift: abundant. The verse displays the blessing: received. Achsah's request for springs and Caleb's gift of the upper and nether springs display the crux: Give me a blessing; for thou hast given me a south land; give me also springs of water. And he gave her the upper springs, and the nether springs (Matthew 7:7; Ephesians 3:20; John 4:14) — the soul that asks of the Father the fuller blessing, not content with the dry land but seeking the springs, and receiving more than asked, a figure of the thirst for the springs of living water and the Father who gives abundantly. The verse displays the cities of the tribe of Judah in the south country (vv.20-21).
